title: End Of The World

jokes.text: Again, Andy, you don't need to bow.
jokes.setup: Andy formally presents someone to Leslie
jokes.punchline: Leslie has to tell him not to bow, implying he's overdoing the formality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy character moment - his earnest over-formality is endearing and fits his childlike enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Leslie Knope, I'd like to present to you... *bows*

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, you don't have to bow.

jokes.text: Turns out, when you think the world is ending, you don't aim so carefully in the Porta-Potties.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains the cult's all-night vigils are annoying
jokes.punchline: The specific gross detail about bathroom hygiene during apocalyptic events
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from 'super annoying' to hilariously specific gross detail. Strong structure and unexpected punchline

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The all-night vigils are annoying, but you know what's really gross? They don't have a sewage system. So during the apocalypse, they're just going to the bathroom in holes in the ground. Holes! In the ground!

jokes.text: Well, they figure if people criticize them, it'll seem like they're attacking something very reasonable. That's weirdly brilliant.
jokes.setup: Ben asks why the cult calls themselves 'The Reasonableists'
jokes.punchline: The twisted logic is so backwards it's actually clever
jokes.explanation: Smart commentary on how manipulative organizations operate. The 'weirdly brilliant' reaction sells the absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Why do they call themselves 'The Reasonableists'?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The twisted logic is so backwards it's actually clever.

jokes.text: They've said that the world is gonna end 15 times, and the only bad thing that's ever happened on any of those dates is Lance Armstrong dumping Sheryl Crow.
jokes.setup: Leslie reassures there's nothing to worry about from the cult's predictions
jokes.punchline: The specific celebrity breakup as the only 'catastrophe'
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection from apocalyptic to trivial celebrity gossip. Dating the episode but works for character perspective

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: There's nothing to worry about. The cult's predictions are never accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Although, they did correctly predict that Brad Pitt and Angelina Jolie would break up.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So maybe we should be concerned.

jokes.text: That was a tragic day. LIVESTRONG. Mmm.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions Lance Armstrong dumping Sheryl Crow
jokes.punchline: Ben's deadpan agreement with Leslie's absurd perspective
jokes.explanation: Ben playing along with Leslie's dramatic perspective. The 'LIVESTRONG' reference adds specificity

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Lance Armstrong dumped Sheryl Crow. Can you believe that? I mean, she's amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's crazy. He's an idiot.

jokes.text: These people live on planet Nutbrain.
jokes.setup: Leslie insists on accompanying the cult members
jokes.punchline: The made-up planet name 'Nutbrain'
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay combining 'nutty' and 'brain.' Fits Leslie's tendency toward creative insults

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going with you. I need to see this made-up planet name 'Nutbrain' for myself.

jokes.text: I live on planet Nut Bran. Bran and nuts are very helpful for your colon.
jokes.setup: Leslie says the cult lives on 'planet Nutbrain'
jokes.punchline: Andy mishears it as health advice about digestive health
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - mishearing an insult as earnest health advice. The colon comment is wonderfully inappropriate

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The cult lives on planet Nutbrain.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Nutbrain? That's actually really good advice. I've been eating too much dairy.

jokes.text: Ben and I don't hang out much these days. Big deal. Lots of people don't hang out. Jerry and April. Obama and Madonna, probably.
jokes.setup: Leslie downplays not hanging out with Ben
jokes.punchline: The escalation from coworkers to random celebrity pairing
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure ending with absurd celebrity comparison. Shows Leslie's defensive rationalization

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben and I hang out all the time. We're very close.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You guys haven't hung out in like three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not true. We grabbed coffee last week.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That was two months ago.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, we text constantly.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You don't text him.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Fine. But if Ben and I were a celebrity couple, we'd be called Lesben, and we'd be huge.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You'd also need Ben to actually know you were in a relationship with him.

jokes.text: You started out with $450,000. Entertainment 720 is dead. It's up in company heaven. Along with Pets.com, Blockbuster, and Ask Jeeves.
jokes.setup: Tom and Jean-Ralphio are excited about getting $5,000 each
jokes.punchline: The list of famously failed companies their business joins
jokes.explanation: Good reality check humor. Ask Jeeves reference is particularly dated but works for the era

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Dude, we're gonna be rich! Five grand each!

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yeah! We're gonna start our own company!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We'll be right up there with the greats.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yeah! Like Enron!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Lehman Brothers!

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Blockbuster!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jamba Juice.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: What? Jamba Juice is still around.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Not the way I manage money.

jokes.text: My company is no better than a company where you ask a fake butler to Google things for you.
jokes.setup: Tom realizes his company is like other failed businesses
jokes.punchline: The specific description of Ask Jeeves as absurdly pointless
jokes.explanation: Clever deconstruction of a real business model. Tom's devastation makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: My company is just like Ask Jeeves. We had a great idea, we were ahead of our time, and now we're completely obsolete and nobody remembers us.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ask Jeeves was a search engine where you asked a butler questions. A butler! For the internet! How did anyone think that was a good idea?

jokes.text: So what's next, Tommy Davidson? I say we invest our 10 large, and then I accidentally get run over by a city bus, and we start our own hip hop label.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio asks what they should do next
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of getting run over by a bus as part of a business plan
jokes.explanation: Pure Jean-Ralphio - treating getting hit by a bus as a business opportunity. Perfectly absurd character logic

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: What should we do next?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: I'm thinking we get hit by a bus, collect the insurance money, and use that to start our business.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's not a business plan.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: What? It's foolproof!

jokes.text: Because I'm in like Lara Flynn Boyle from The Practice.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio agrees to throw one last party
jokes.punchline: The obscure and dated TV reference to express enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Typical Jean-Ralphio random pop culture reference. Works for character but feels lazy

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yo, this party's gonna be straight up Fire Marshal Bill!

jokes.text: Diddly, diddly, diddly, diddly, diddly, diddly, swag.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio is expressing enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: The nonsensical vocal riff ending with 'swag'
jokes.explanation: Pure Jean-Ralphio character moment but not particularly clever. Random = funny approach

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yo, yo, yo, yo, yo! Swag!

jokes.text: You watch me play Xbox. And then I can watch you make some prank phone calls. We get hammered, make out.
jokes.setup: Andy asks what they should do tonight
jokes.punchline: April's deadpan description of their routine romantic evening
jokes.explanation: Perfect April delivery - romantic activities described with complete emotional detachment

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What should we do tonight?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We could go to Sweetums and get a pretzel. Then we could go home and watch TV until we fall asleep. Then tomorrow we wake up and do it all over again until we die.

jokes.text: That's because repetition is the key to a good marriage.
jokes.setup: April says they do the same thing every night
jokes.punchline: Andy's earnest but misguided relationship wisdom
jokes.explanation: Andy trying to sound wise but revealing he completely misunderstands relationships

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We do the same thing every night.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's what relationships are, April. It's about finding someone you love and doing the same thing over and over until one of you dies.

jokes.text: I'd like to remake the movie Kazaam with Shaquille O'Neal where he plays a genie, and I'd like to get it right.
jokes.setup: Andy lists items from his bucket list
jokes.punchline: The specific terrible movie he wants to remake and 'get right'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific and absurd. The phrase 'get it right' implies the original had potential. Peak Andy logic

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I want to remake 'Son of the Mask' and get it right this time.

jokes.text: When people are walking by, be like this. [visual gag of Andy demonstrating snooty airplane behavior]
jokes.setup: Andy wants to fly first class
jokes.punchline: His demonstration of how to act snooty on a plane
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy showing Andy's misconception of how wealthy people behave

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm gonna show you how to act classy on a plane. First, you gotta lean back like this and say 'These pretzels are making me thirsty,' but like, super annoyed about it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Then when the flight attendant comes by, you go 'Excuse me, do you have any other nuts? These are so pedestrian.' Even though you love pretzels and you're gonna eat all of them.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And when you land, you take off your sunglasses real slow and say 'That was a bumpy flight. I'm used to private jets.' You're not used to private jets.

jokes.text: Whoa! Still a lot left over.
jokes.setup: They have $18.04 left after withdrawing $1000
jokes.punchline: Andy's sarcastic appreciation of the remaining money
jokes.explanation: Simple sarcasm but fits Andy's character. Not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Eighteen dollars and four cents. That's... that's basically a million dollars.

jokes.text: Yes! Now, this is what I imagined.
jokes.setup: Andy was disappointed by $1000, then April made it into smaller bills
jokes.punchline: His excitement over the same money in a different form
jokes.explanation: Shows Andy's childlike thinking - presentation matters more than substance

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, you broke it into smaller bills?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's the same thousand dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No it's not! Look at all this money!

jokes.text: Have you ever seen this much cash in your entire life? I just handed it to you.
jokes.setup: Andy asks April if she's ever seen this much cash
jokes.punchline: April's deadpan reminder that she literally just gave it to him
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline with April's characteristic bluntness deflating Andy's excitement

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Have you ever seen this much cash?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I literally just gave it to you.

jokes.text: Nickels. I want nickels. A billion nickels!
jokes.setup: Andy is excited about having cash
jokes.punchline: His escalation to wanting an impractical amount of the least valuable coin
jokes.explanation: Andy's childlike fixation on the novelty rather than practicality of money

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I have cash! Real money in my pocket!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what I could do with this? I could buy a soda. Or two sodas!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, forget sodas. I'm gonna use this to get pennies. Thousands of pennies.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna fill a bathtub with pennies and just roll around in them like I'm some kind of copper-based Scrooge McDuck.

jokes.text: The Zorpies are ridiculous. But like the Founding Fathers, I believe in absolute freedom of religion. Also their ceremonies require the playing of flutes.
jokes.setup: Ron explains his tolerant attitude toward the cult
jokes.punchline: The reveal that his tolerance is financially motivated
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - principled stance undermined by capitalist opportunity. Great misdirection

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Look, I don't care what people believe in. If they want to worship a golden calf or think the government is run by lizard people, that's their business.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm a live-and-let-live kind of man.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's very progressive of you, Ron.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Well, they also gave me a significant tax write-off.

jokes.text: Flutes are $80 apiece, and recorders are $150.
jokes.setup: Ron mentions making flutes for the ceremony
jokes.punchline: The specific pricing structure for musical instruments
jokes.explanation: Ron the entrepreneur. The specific prices make it funnier and more believable

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm making flutes for the ceremony. I have three sizes: small, medium, and large. Small flute costs $50, medium flute costs $100, and large flute costs $150.

jokes.text: Would you take a check? You can cash it tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Herb wants to buy flutes from Ron
jokes.punchline: Offering to pay with a check that would be useless after the world ends
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about the inconsistency in apocalyptic thinking. Shows cult member's confusion about logistics

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Ron, I'd like to buy those flutes from you.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: They're not for sale.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: I'll write you a check.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A check?

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Yes, post-dated for after the world ends.

jokes.text: The entire party is a VIP area. There's also a double VIP area. A triple VIP area. And a Centurion Club Elite VIP area. Sponsored by SoBe Lifewater. No one is allowed in there. Not even us.
jokes.setup: Tom describes the party's VIP structure
jokes.punchline: The escalation to an area so VIP that even the hosts can't enter
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure showing Tom's over-the-top party planning. The sponsorship detail adds authenticity

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We have a VIP section, obviously. But then we have a Super VIP section. And then we have an Ultra VIP section. And then we have a section that's so VIP, that even I can't go in there.

jokes.text: What up, Keith? He actually once tossed me out of a club three years ago. Water under the bridge.
jokes.setup: Tom mentions hiring scary bouncers
jokes.punchline: Casually greeting the bouncer who previously ejected him
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's ability to network even with people who've wronged him. Character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey, how's it going?

dialogue.speaker: Bouncer
dialogue.line: You're banned.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Right, right. Well, I'm actually the owner now, so...

jokes.text: Oh, thank God. No one shows up to a good party on time. If anyone had actually shown up right now, the whole party would have been ruined. Would have been a disaster.
jokes.setup: No one shows up to Tom's party immediately
jokes.punchline: Tom's desperate rationalization that this is actually perfect
jokes.explanation: Tom's defensive spin on obvious failure. The repetition emphasizes his desperation

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This is actually perfect. Now I can set up all the surprises before everyone gets here.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Plus, I get some alone time to mentally prepare myself for all the socializing.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know what? This is the best party I've ever thrown.

jokes.text: It's a little long. Okay. Zorp Shmorp. Doomsday Prediction Falls Flat as Citizens Spend Pleasant Evening Enjoying One of Pawnee's Finest Parks. Somehow longer.
jokes.setup: Shauna thinks her first headline is too long
jokes.punchline: Her 'shorter' alternative is actually much longer
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke about bad headline writing. Leslie's observation makes it land

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: No, no, no. That's too long. How about: 'Local Woman Initiates Positive Community Change Through Dedicated Civic Engagement and Strategic Partnership Development'?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's longer.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: Well, it's more accurate.

jokes.text: He's a man, and he's a worker, and he is... We've never discussed sex, so... We've always just been very business-like, so your guess is as good, nay, better than mine.
jokes.setup: Shauna asks about Ben's relationship status
jokes.punchline: Leslie's increasingly awkward non-answer revealing her discomfort
jokes.explanation: Leslie's awkwardness when discussing Ben shows her suppressed feelings. The formal language makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, Ben is... he's a great guy. Really great. And he's, you know, very capable. Competent. He has two hands and two feet, which is the standard amount. And he's never once tried to murder me, which I think speaks volumes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So yeah, we're just... we're colleagues. Professional colleagues who sometimes eat lunch in the same room. Coincidentally. Because the cafeteria only has so many tables.

jokes.text: She smiles, and then they fall in love, and then they get married, and then she changes her name to Shauna Malwae-Wyatt. Or he's going to be really progressive and change his name to Ben Wyatt Malwae-Tweep. God, I am so annoyed that he would hypothetically do that.
jokes.setup: Leslie is jealous watching Shauna and Ben talk
jokes.punchline: Getting angry at Ben for something completely hypothetical and progressively specific
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Leslie's spiraling jealousy. The specific name combinations and 'hypothetically' anger are perfect

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Why are you even talking to her? And don't say 'because she asked me a question' because that's a terrible reason.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What if you guys become friends? What if you become best friends? What if you decide to start a business together?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A bed and breakfast in the Poconos. What if it's very successful? What if you're on the cover of Innkeeper Monthly?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What if you fall in love? What if you get married on the cover of that same magazine?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And I'm not invited because I'm too busy being angry at you for ruining my life!

jokes.text: Oh, my God. I know that move. They're going to have sex in five minutes.
jokes.setup: Leslie watches Shauna and Ben interact
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic overinterpretation of normal conversation
jokes.explanation: Leslie's jealous paranoia making her see romance where none exists. The specific timeframe is funny

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my god, did you see that? The way he looked at her when she said 'the report is due Friday'? That's not just eye contact, Ben, that's a CONNECTION. She tucked her hair behind her ear. He blinked. Twice. That's basically a marriage proposal in body language. They're soulmates, I can feel it. We need to start planning the wedding. I'm thinking outdoor ceremony, string lights, maybe at that barn upstate...

jokes.text: Oh, yeah! 10 bucks. That counts as winning the lottery.
jokes.setup: Andy finds money on the ground
jokes.punchline: His decision that this fulfills his lottery-winning bucket list item
jokes.explanation: Andy's low standards for achievement. Good callback to his bucket list

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my God, twenty bucks! This is it. This is my lottery ticket. I'm gonna be rich!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, actually, you know what? I found money on the ground. That was on my bucket list. Bucket list item: complete. I'm basically a lottery winner.

jokes.text: Honey, my bucket list doesn't say, 'Make a pretty awesome grilled cheese sandwich.' We gotta go back to the store.
jokes.setup: April says the sandwich is pretty awesome
jokes.punchline: Andy's perfectionist approach to his bucket list achievement
jokes.explanation: Andy taking his ridiculous goals seriously. Shows his earnest commitment to meaningless achievements

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: The sandwich is pretty awesome.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Awesome? April, this isn't just awesome. This is a bucket list item. I've been planning this moment for years. The bread has to be artisanal, the meat has to be heritage breed, and the condiments have to be applied with the precision of a Swiss watchmaker.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's a sandwich, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's not a sandwich, April. It's a SANDWICH. And I'm going to enjoy every single moment of this achievement.

jokes.text: Keep your pants on. What? I mean, keep your pants on, girl. I mean, those are really nice pants. I really like your pants. Where did you get them?
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to interrupt Ben and Shauna's conversation
jokes.punchline: Her awkward recovery from an inappropriate comment by fixating on pants
jokes.explanation: Leslie's desperation making her say increasingly awkward things. The escalation to pants obsession is funny

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hey guys, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Oh, just about Shauna's new project.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's great, Shauna. You look really... fertile today.

dialogue.speaker: Shauna
dialogue.line: Um, what?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I mean— your pants! Those are great pants. Very nice pants. I like the pants. The way they're on you. The pants.

jokes.text: Because you can't go. I mean, what if you're not here when Zorp shows up? Boy, would your face be red.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to keep Ben from leaving
jokes.punchline: Using embarrassment as a concern for someone whose face would be melted off
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about apocalyptic expectations vs social embarrassment. Leslie's desperation shows through

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, you can't leave! What about us? What about everything we've built together?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, I have to go.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But think about how embarrassed you'll be!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: My face would be melted off.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Exactly! So embarrassing!

jokes.text: When Zorp shows up, your faces will be melted off and used as fuel.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions faces being red
jokes.punchline: Herb's matter-of-fact correction about the actual apocalyptic process
jokes.explanation: Herb's casual delivery of horrific apocalyptic details. Good contrast with Leslie's concern about embarrassment

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Their faces are all red.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Actually, in a nuclear apocalypse, the faces would turn white from the flash burn, then blister and peel. The red comes later, after the initial radiation sickness sets in.

jokes.text: Wow, what an unbelievably unpleasant person.
jokes.setup: Herb describes faces being melted off
jokes.punchline: Ben's understated reaction to cosmic horror
jokes.explanation: Good understatement calling apocalyptic prophet merely 'unpleasant.' Typical Ben rationality

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Their faces just melted off. Like wax.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's not great.

jokes.text: It's a grand experiment, and I am a party scientist.
jokes.setup: Tom explains his philosophy about perfect parties
jokes.punchline: Describing himself as a 'party scientist'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom pomposity - elevating party planning to scientific pursuit. Very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm not just a party planner, I'm a party scientist. I have a philosophy about parties. The key to a perfect party is that the party must start at the exact right time. If people arrive even five minutes early, the party hasn't started yet. But if they arrive five minutes late, the party is already over. It's all about precision.

jokes.text: That was Symphony for the Righteous Destruction of Humanity in E Minor by the late Lou Presotovich.
jokes.setup: Ron introduces a musical piece
jokes.punchline: The overly specific and apocalyptic classical music title
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific fake classical music title. The formal presentation of apocalyptic theme is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'd like to play a piece for you. It's called 'Requiem for a Thousand Exploding Suns, Opus 47 in B-Flat Minor.'

jokes.text: And full disclosure, I think they're bonkersville.
jokes.setup: Leslie tells Herb she read his books
jokes.punchline: Her diplomatic way of calling him crazy using a made-up word
jokes.explanation: Leslie trying to be polite while calling someone crazy. 'Bonkersville' is a nice character touch

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I read your books. They're very... imaginative.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, you're a real... what's the word... oh, you're bananicorn.

jokes.text: That of a fleshless, chattering skeleton when Zorp the Surveyor arrives and burns your flesh off with his volcano mouth.
jokes.setup: Herb explains reincarnation after death
jokes.punchline: The specific gruesome description of Zorp's method
jokes.explanation: 'Volcano mouth' and 'chattering skeleton' are vivid and ridiculous. Good contrast with peaceful reincarnation talk

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: When you die, you get reincarnated as another life form based on how you lived.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: You live a good life, you come back as something better. You live a bad life, you come back as something worse.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Zorp lived a bad life. So he came back as a sinkhole.

jokes.text: That was very strange, what you just said. I don't like it as much as that other thing you said.
jokes.setup: Herb describes horrific apocalyptic transformation
jokes.punchline: Leslie's measured, diplomatic reaction to cosmic horror
jokes.explanation: Leslie's diplomatic understatement in response to apocalyptic horror. Her politeness is absurd in context

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, that sounds bad, but I think if we just get the Parks Department involved, we can probably find a solution that works for everyone.

jokes.text: Do you want to play a fun game with me that I made up called ChessRisk? It's half-chess, half-Risk, and it takes 15 hours to play.
jokes.setup: Leslie desperately tries to keep Ben from leaving
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous hybrid game designed to be as time-consuming as possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie invention - combining two notoriously long games. The 15-hour duration shows her desperation

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, wait! I have an idea. What if we played a game together? A game so epic, so all-consuming, that you'll forget all about leaving Pawnee.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, what kind of game?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Calvinball meets Catan meets fantasy football meets competitive bird watching. I call it... Caltanbird.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That sounds horrible.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It gets better! Each turn takes six hours, and no one's ever actually won. The rules change every five minutes, and half of them are written in Esperanto.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! What? I forgot I have a thing I need to show you. Oh. I need to bring you there right now. It's so amazing. It's going to freak you out.
jokes.setup: Ben tries to leave despite Leslie's game offer
jokes.punchline: Leslie's increasingly frantic improvisation of fake emergencies
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's desperation but not particularly clever. The escalation works for character

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, Ben, don't go! We could play a game!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, I really need to get back to work.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, wait — there's a gas leak! A gas leak in the building!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, no, scratch that — your car is being towed. Your car is being towed right now.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, okay — the mayor is here. The mayor is here and he's asking for you specifically.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, there's a swarm of bees! A biblical swarm of bees!

jokes.text: Just crazy enough to work. Help!
jokes.setup: Andy suggests a fake hostage scenario
jokes.punchline: April's deadpan agreement followed by immediate commitment to the bit
jokes.explanation: April's willingness to immediately commit to Andy's crazy schemes. Good character moment

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We could do a fake hostage scenario.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm going to shoot you now.

jokes.text: My face is fine, Mikhail Petrov.
jokes.setup: Andy is doing an action movie scene
jokes.punchline: The random Russian name he gives the 'villain'
jokes.explanation: Andy's action movie fantasy includes specific character names. Shows his commitment to the roleplay

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, so I'm doing this action movie scene, right? And I need a villain name. A Russian villain name. So I'm like... *Dimitri Volkoff*. No wait, *Sergei Petrov*. Actually, you know what? *Yuri Boombottom*.

jokes.text: He's stolen my jewels. And now, he's going to ravish my body. And he stinks! He really stinks!
jokes.setup: April plays the damsel in distress
jokes.punchline: The escalation from jewel theft to ravishment to the prosaic complaint about smell
jokes.explanation: Good escalation ending with mundane complaint. April committing to the bit while staying in character

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Help! I've been kidnapped by jewel thieves!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Now they're going to ravish me!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh God, and they smell like a bus station bathroom.

jokes.text: Looks like this Siberian husky is going off to jail. Russian off to jail.
jokes.setup: Andy delivers his action hero punchline
jokes.punchline: The terrible pun and April having to correct his delivery
jokes.explanation: Bad pun made funnier by Andy's delivery problems and April's help. Shows their dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm like an action hero, but instead of bullets, I use... compliments. I'm gonna go give everybody a piece of my mind. And by piece of my mind, I mean a compliment. Boom. Nailed it.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's not how any of this works.

jokes.text: Hey, you know what? Can I go? Gayle is making a roast.
jokes.setup: Jerry has been participating in the elaborate roleplay scenario
jokes.punchline: He politely excuses himself mid-action sequence for dinner
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry moment - prioritizing domestic comfort over adventure. Great timing and character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, excuse me, I need to head out for dinner.

jokes.text: This gas station was owned by Mick Jagger.
jokes.setup: Leslie brings Ben to a 'special place'
jokes.punchline: The anticlimactic reveal of a mundane location with dubious rock history
jokes.explanation: Leslie's enthusiasm for obscure local history. The mundane location makes her excitement absurd

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, I've brought you to a very special place. This rock right here? The founding fathers definitely walked past this exact spot. Probably. On their way to somewhere important.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, this is just a parking lot behind a Arby's.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A HISTORIC parking lot behind a Arby's. Now come on, I want to show you where I think Ben Franklin might have tied his horse.

jokes.text: But I think the most likely explanation is that legendary Rolling Stones front man Mick Jagger owned this gas station from 1951 to 1958.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains her research about M. Jagger
jokes.punchline: The completely implausible timeline that would make Mick Jagger a child gas station owner
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie logic - thorough research leading to impossible conclusions. The specific dates make it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: According to my research, Mick Jagger was born in 1943, which would make him only 8 years old when he opened his first gas station in 1951.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, that doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, that's what the internet says!

jokes.text: I know what you're doing, Leslie. You know, you can't do this. You know, we broke up. And I kind of feel like we shouldn't hang out together, just the two of us. Because every time we do, it just makes it harder. You know?
jokes.setup: Leslie's desperate attempts to keep Ben around
jokes.punchline: Ben's honest, vulnerable response cutting through the comedy
jokes.explanation: Shifts from comedy to emotional honesty. The cringe comes from the situation, not the dialogue

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You can't leave Pawnee. I won't let you. You're the only one who understands me.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, I love you. But I can't stay just because you need me to. That's not fair to either of us.

jokes.text: So I'll steal my dad's car. Look, this is a stupid idea, but right at this exact second, we have enough momentum to do something stupid before we realize how stupid it is.
jokes.setup: Andy wants to go to Mount Rushmore
jokes.punchline: April's self-aware analysis of their impulsive decision-making
jokes.explanation: April being surprisingly wise about stupid decisions. Meta-commentary on impulsive behavior

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Let's go to Mount Rushmore!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're going to Mount Rushmore aren't we? Even though we have no plan, no money, and it's a nine hour drive.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yep.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: And we're leaving right now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Right now.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: This is the worst decision we've ever made and we're both fully aware of it.

jokes.text: Socket wrench sounds pretty good, actually.
jokes.setup: Discussion of reincarnation possibilities including socket wrench
jokes.punchline: Ron's genuine appreciation for being reincarnated as a tool
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron - preferring to be a useful tool rather than human. Concise and character-perfect

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: If I were reincarnated, I would want to come back as a socket wrench. It's useful, it's reliable, and it doesn't have to deal with people's emotions.

jokes.text: Well, I'm a practicing none of your...business.
jokes.setup: Someone asks what religion Ron is
jokes.punchline: His polite but firm deflection of personal questions
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron privacy stance delivered as a fake religion. Great structure and character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm not going to discuss my personal beliefs with you. That's between me and whoever I pray to, which is nobody. I don't pray. I don't believe in god. But if I did, I wouldn't tell you about it.

jokes.text: I actually forgot they ever dated. I was trying to hit that.
jokes.setup: Jean-Ralphio calls Lucy for Tom
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he forgot about their relationship because of his own interest
jokes.explanation: Jean-Ralphio's selfishness accidentally doing something nice. The crude language fits character

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Hey Lucy, it's Jean-Ralphio. Tom wanted me to call you.

dialogue.speaker: Lucy
dialogue.line: Oh my god, finally! Where has he been? We've been dating for three months!

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Dating? Oh right, right. Yeah, so Tom's been super busy with the business stuff. He asked me to tell you he's gonna be late.

dialogue.speaker: Lucy
dialogue.line: Late? Late for what? We had plans tonight!

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Oh, well he wanted me to let you know that... wait, you guys are dating? Since when?

dialogue.speaker: Lucy
dialogue.line: Since May! Your best friend's been in a relationship with me for three months and you forgot?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Three months? Man, that's when I got really into snake charming. I've been so focused on that, I totally forgot.

jokes.text: Dawn is nigh. Zorp the Surveyor approaches, and all earthly debts will be repaid. Last call for donuts.
jokes.setup: Herb makes apocalyptic pronouncement
jokes.punchline: The mundane donut announcement immediately following cosmic drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect juxtaposition of cosmic drama with snack logistics. Shows the practical side of apocalypse cults

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: The end times are upon us. Civilization as we know it will crumble to dust. Famine, pestilence, and darkness will consume the earth.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: We have donuts in the break room.

jokes.text: If the world was ending tomorrow, I'd want to be with him. Well, that's significant. The problem is, Leslie, the world is not ending tomorrow. The sun's going to rise right over there. It will be a regular Friday, and you'll be in the exact same position you were in before.
jokes.setup: Leslie confesses her feelings about Ben
jokes.punchline: Ron's blunt reality check about tomorrow being just Friday
jokes.explanation: Ron cutting through emotional drama with practical wisdom. 'Regular Friday' is perfectly understated

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, I have to tell you something. I think I'm in love with Ben.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's nice. Tomorrow's Friday though.

jokes.text: If it makes you feel any better, Leslie, we'll all be dead in 20 minutes. That does make me feel better. Thank you, Herb.
jokes.setup: Leslie is miserable about her situation
jokes.punchline: Finding comfort in apocalyptic predictions and genuinely thanking the prophet
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about finding comfort in death. Leslie's polite response to apocalyptic comfort is funny

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm so miserable. Everything is terrible and nothing will ever get better.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Well, the world is going to end in 2012. Might as well enjoy yourself now.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thank you. That actually makes me feel so much better.

jokes.text: I tell you what. You play whatever you want. I'm just going to take a real quick snooze for, like, 12 hours so I'm fresh.
jokes.setup: Andy and April are driving 30 hours to Mount Rushmore
jokes.punchline: Andy's plan to sleep for most of the drive while April does all the driving
jokes.explanation: Andy's selfish approach to their romantic adventure. The 'so I'm fresh' justification is perfect

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, I'm gonna sleep for like the first 28 hours, okay? You're gonna do all the driving.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: What? No. We agreed we'd both drive.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know, but I'm really tired. Plus, I get car sick when I'm awake in a car.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You literally just slept for eight hours.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, but that was sleep sleep. This is gonna be drive sleep. Totally different thing.

jokes.text: Shauna Malwae-Tweep thinks you're cute. What? That's why I took you to Mick Jagger's abandoned gas station. Because she thinks you're cute, and I was afraid you guys were going to make out and have babies.
jokes.setup: Leslie confesses her scheming to Ben
jokes.punchline: The escalation from making out directly to having babies
jokes.explanation: Leslie's irrational jealousy making her skip steps. Good callback to the gas station scene

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I may have orchestrated our entire relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I created situations where we'd run into each other, I may have sabotaged some of your dates, and I definitely planted that article about you in the Pawnee Journal.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But it worked! We're together now. We're making out. Next thing you know we'll be having babies and naming them after government buildings.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... a very specific escalation.

jokes.text: Oh, well, Shauna is not here. Oh. See what I did? I do. I'm very sneaky.
jokes.setup: Leslie apologizes to both Ben and Shauna
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Leslie made up Shauna's interest and her pride in her deception
jokes.explanation: Leslie's pride in her manipulative behavior. Shows her competitive nature even in personal matters

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, Ben. I'm sorry, Shauna. I made up your entire interest in model trains. I invented it all. I literally created a false person.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, what?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And I'm actually really proud of how convincing I was.

jokes.text: Where's all the faces? Like the presidents.
jokes.setup: Andy and April reach Mount Rushmore
jokes.punchline: Andy's confusion about not seeing the famous presidential faces from their viewpoint
jokes.explanation: Andy's literal thinking about famous landmarks. Shows he didn't think through the logistics

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, where are the faces? I thought Mount Rushmore would be... bigger. And, you know, have faces on it.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're on the wrong side of the mountain, dummy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that would explain why I don't see George Washington's beautiful face right now.

jokes.text: Well, math is hard.
jokes.setup: Herb admits to making crucial errors in his apocalypse calculations
jokes.punchline: Leslie's dismissive response to cosmic miscalculation
jokes.explanation: Leslie's casual dismissal of apocalyptic calculation errors. Good understatement

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Herb, you had one job. ONE JOB. And that job was to not mess up the calculations for the end of the world.

jokes.text: Um, oh, on the 19th, we can't give you the park. We have a Spring Spectacular free ice cream giveaway. I misspoke. Yeah, it's May 20. That is free. Okay. Thanks. End of world. May 20th.
jokes.setup: Herb wants to reschedule the apocalypse
jokes.punchline: Leslie treating the end of the world like a routine park booking and Herb adjusting accordingly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Parks and Rec bureaucratic humor. Treating apocalypse scheduling like any other civic event is brilliant

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: I need to reschedule the apocalypse.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, let me check the calendar. We have the end of the world penciled in for Tuesday at 2 PM. Would Wednesday work better for you?

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Wednesday is fine, but I'll need the permits filed by Friday.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No problem. I'll get you the apocalypse application. It's the same form as the petting zoo, just with more fire.

tmdb_overview: A local Pawnee group predicts the end of days, causing chaos in the Parks department.
insight: Doomsday panic spawns 65 jokes in 22 minutes—absurdist chaos over crafted setup.
